docs.title: RCS Radar Signature Toolbox
docs.text: A comprehensive Python toolbox for generating STL meshes from NURBS surfaces and computing Radar Cross Section (RCS) using Physical Optics and Boundary Element Methods.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• STL File Handling: Read, visualize, and generate STL mesh files
  • NURBS Support: Create STL meshes from NURBS surface definitions (sphere, cylinder, cone, torus, box, custom)
  • RCS Computation:
  • Physical Optics (PO) method
  • Fast BEM (simplified facet-based)
  • Full BEM Method of Moments (MoM)
  • Visualization: Plot RCS patterns in Cartesian, polar, and 2D contour formats

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install numpy matplotlib stl geomdl

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Generate a sphere STL
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape sphere --output sphere.stl

# Compute RCS
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --cut --save rcs_data.npz

# Plot results
python plot_bem_rcs.py --input rcs_data.npz --output plots/

docs.title: Available Scripts
docs.text: Script Description read_stl.py Read and display STL file information plot_stl.py Visualize STL mesh in 3D nurbs_to_stl.py Generate STL from NURBS definitions compute_rcs.py Compute RCS using Physical Optics bem_rcs.py Compute RCS using BEM methods plot_bem_rcs.py Plot RCS results

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
/opt/dev/Qiskit/
├── read_stl.py          # STL reader
├── plot_stl.py          # STL visualizer  
├── nurbs_to_stl.py      # NURBS to STL converter
├── compute_rcs.py       # PO RCS calculator
├── bem_rcs.py           # BEM RCS calculator
├── plot_bem_rcs.py      # RCS plotter
├── mkdocs.yml           # Documentation config
└── docs/                # Documentation files

docs.title: Radar Cross Section
docs.text:
Radar Cross Section (RCS) measures how detectable an object is by radar. It's measured in square meters (m²) or decibels relative to a square meter (dBsm):

$$\sigma_{dBsm} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_{m^2})$$

docs.title: Physical Optics (PO)
docs.text: PO is a high-frequency approximation that assumes currents on illuminated surfaces are twice the incident magnetic field. It's fast but less accurate for complex shapes.

docs.title: Boundary Element Method (BEM)
docs.text: BEM (Method of Moments) solves integral equations for surface currents. More accurate than PO but computationally expensive (O(n³) complexity).

docs.title: Generate and Compute RCS for a Sphere
docs.text:
# Generate sphere mesh
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape sphere --radius 0.5 --samples 40 -o sphere.stl

# View mesh
python plot_stl.py sphere.stl sphere_plot.png

# Compute RCS cut
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --freq 10e9 --cut -s rcs.npz

# Plot results
python plot_bem_rcs.py -i rcs.npz -o plots/

docs.title: Compute 2D RCS Map
docs.text:
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --map --theta-res 91 --phi-res 91 -s rcs_map.npz
python plot_bem_rcs.py -i rcs_map.npz -o plots/

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT License

docs.title: Compute RCS with BEM
docs.text: Compute Radar Cross Section (RCS) using Boundary Element Method (BEM).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python bem_rcs.py <stl_file> [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description Default stl_file Input STL file (required) - --freq Frequency in Hz 10e9 (10 GHz) --method BEM method (fast/full) fast --cut Compute theta cut 0-180° False --map Compute 2D theta-phi map False --theta-res Theta resolution 91 --phi-res Phi resolution 91 --theta Theta angle (degrees) 0.0 --phi Phi angle (degrees) 0.0 --polarization Polarization (vv/hh) vv --save, -s Save results to .npz file -

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Fast BEM - single angle
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --freq 10e9 --theta 0

# Fast BEM - theta cut
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --cut --save rcs_cut.npz

# Fast BEM - 2D map
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --map --theta-res 91 --phi-res 91 --save rcs_map.npz

# Full BEM (Method of Moments)
python bem_rcs.py sphere.stl --method full --cut

docs.title: Fast BEM
docs.text: Simplified facet-based BEM using physical optics with improved phase handling. Suitable for quick estimates.

docs.title: Full BEM (MoM)
docs.text: Method of Moments implementation with RWG basis functions. More accurate but computationally expensive (O(n³)).

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Loading: sphere.stl
Frequency: 10.00 GHz
FastBEM: 1600 triangles loaded

Computing RCS cut...

RCS Results:
  θ=0°:   45.56 dBsm
  θ=90°:  45.56 dBsm
  θ=180°: 45.56 dBsm

docs.title: Boundary Element Method
docs.text:
BEM solves the Electric Field Integral Equation (EFIE):

$$\hat{n} \times E^i = \frac{j}{k} \eta \oint J G ds$$

where: - (E^i) = incident electric field - (J) = induced surface current - (G) = Green's function - (k) = wavenumber - (\eta) = intrinsic impedance

docs.title: RWG Basis Functions
docs.text: The full BEM uses Rao-Wilton-Glisson (RWG) basis functions on triangular elements to represent surface currents.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Method Time Complexity Memory Fast BEM O(n) O(n) Full BEM O(n³) O(n²)

where n = number of facets

docs.title: Save Format
docs.text:
Results are saved in NumPy's .npz format containing:

np.savez('rcs.npz',
          theta=theta_deg,      # theta values in degrees
          phi=phi_deg,          # phi values in degrees (for maps)
          rcs=rcs_db,           # RCS in dBsm
          frequency=freq,       # frequency in Hz
          polarization='vv')    # polarization

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from bem_rcs import FastBEMRCS, BEMRCS, rcs_to_dbsm

# Fast BEM
bem = FastBEMRCS('sphere.stl', frequency=10e9)

# Single angle
rcs = bem.compute_rcs(theta=0.0, phi=0.0, polarization='vv')

# Theta cut
theta_range = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 91)
rcs_values = bem.compute_rcs_cut(theta_range, phi=0.0)

# 2D Map
theta_range = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 91)
phi_range = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 91)
rcs_map = bem.compute_rcs_map(theta_range, phi_range)

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Full BEM is slow for meshes with >500 triangles
  • Requires significant memory for large problems
  • Accuracy depends on mesh resolution relative to wavelength

docs.title: Compute RCS with Physical Optics
docs.text: Compute Radar Cross Section (RCS) using the Physical Optics (PO) approximation.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python compute_rcs.py <stl_file> [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description Default stl_file Input STL file (required) - --freq Frequency in Hz 10e9 (10 GHz) --polarization Polarization (vv/hh) vv --theta Theta angle (degrees) 0.0 --phi Phi angle (degrees) 0.0 --cut Compute theta cut 0-180° False

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Single angle
python compute_rcs.py sphere.stl --freq 10e9 --theta 45 --phi 30

# Theta cut (0-180 degrees)
python compute_rcs.py sphere.stl --cut

# Different polarization
python compute_rcs.py sphere.stl --cut --polarization hh

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Loading: sphere.stl
Frequency: 10.00 GHz
Wavelength: 3.00 cm
Triangles: 1600
Total area: 3.1255 m²

Computing RCS cut at φ=0.0° (VV)...

RCS Results:
  θ=0°   (boresight): 41.80 dBsm
  θ=90°  (broadside): 41.84 dBsm
  θ=180° (back):      41.80 dBsm

docs.title: Physical Optics Theory
docs.text:
Physical Optics (PO) is a high-frequency asymptotic method that approximates the surface currents on an illuminated object:

$$J = 2 \hat{n} \times H_i$$

where: - (J) = induced surface current - (\hat{n}) = surface normal - (H_i) = incident magnetic field

The scattered field is computed by integrating the induced currents over the surface.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Fast computation (O(n) where n = number of facets)
  • Works well for smooth, large objects relative to wavelength

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Less accurate for shadowed regions
  • Poor for thin structures or edges
  • Not valid for resonant-sized objects

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from compute_rcs import RadarSignature, rcs_to_dbsm

# Initialize
radar = RadarSignature('sphere.stl', frequency=10e9)

# Single angle RCS
rcs = radar.compute_rcs(theta=np.pi/4, phi=np.pi/6, polarization='vv')

# Convert to dBsm
rcs_db = rcs_to_dbsm(rcs)

# Theta cut
theta_range = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 181)
rcs_values = radar.compute_rcs_cut(theta_range, phi=0.0)

docs.title: RCS Units
docs.text:
• Linear: square meters (m²)
  • Decibels relative to 1 m²: dBsm

$$RCS_{dBsm} = 10 \log_{10}(RCS_{m^2})$$

docs.title: Reference Values
docs.text: Object RCS (m²) RCS (dBsm) Sphere (r=0.5m) 0.785 -1.05 Plate (1m×1m) 1.0 0.0 Car 10-100 10-20 Fighter jet 0.1-5 -10 to 7

docs.title: Generate STL from NURBS
docs.text: Generate STL mesh files from NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline) surface definitions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape <type> [options]

docs.title: Shape Options
docs.text: Shape Description sphere NURBS sphere cylinder NURBS cylinder cone NURBS cone torus NURBS torus box NURBS box (6 connected surfaces) wave Custom wavy surface

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description Default --shape, -s Shape type sphere --output, -o Output STL file output.stl --radius Radius (sphere, cylinder, cone) 0.5 --height Height (cylinder, cone) 1.0 --major-radius Major radius (torus) 0.5 --minor-radius Minor radius (torus) 0.15 --size Box dimensions (X Y Z) 1.0 1.0 1.0 --samples Surface sampling resolution 40

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Generate sphere
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape sphere --radius 0.5 -o sphere.stl

# Generate cylinder
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape cylinder --radius 0.3 --height 2.0 -o cylinder.stl

# Generate torus
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape torus --major-radius 0.8 --minor-radius 0.2 -o torus.stl

# Generate box
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape box --size 2.0 1.0 0.5 -o flat_box.stl

# High resolution sphere
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape sphere --samples 60 -o high_res_sphere.stl

docs.title: Sphere
docs.text:
• Degree: 2 in both U and V directions
  • Control points: 5×5 grid

docs.title: Cylinder
docs.text:
• Degree: 2 in U, 1 in V
  • Control points: 5×2 grid

docs.title: Cone
docs.text:
• Degree: 2 in U, 1 in V
  • Control points: 5×2 grid

docs.title: Torus
docs.text:
• Degree: 2 in both U and V directions
  • Control points: 5×5 grid

docs.title: Box
docs.text:
• Degree: 1 (linear) in both directions
  • 6 surfaces (one per face)

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from nurbs_to_stl import (
    create_nurbs_sphere,
    create_nurbs_cylinder,
    create_nurbs_cone,
    create_nurbs_torus,
    create_nurbs_box,
    create_custom_nurbs_surface,
    nurbs_to_stl
)

# Create sphere surface
surf = create_nurbs_sphere(radius=0.5, u_samples=40, v_samples=40)

# Convert to STL
nurbs_to_stl(surf, 'output.stl')

docs.title: Surface Sampling
docs.text:
The --samples parameter controls mesh resolution: - Higher values = more triangles = smoother surface - Lower values = fewer triangles = faster computation

Samples Approx Triangles 20 ~1,500 40 ~6,000 60 ~13,500 100 ~37,500

docs.title: Plot RCS Results
docs.text: Visualize Radar Cross Section (RCS) computation results from BEM or PO computations.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python plot_bem_rcs.py [options]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description Default --input, -i Input .npz data file - --output, -o Output directory . --title Plot title RCS Pattern

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Plot saved RCS cut
python plot_bem_rcs.py --input rcs_cut.npz --output plots/

# Plot saved 2D RCS map
python plot_bem_rcs.py --input rcs_map.npz --output plots/

# With custom title
python plot_bem_rcs.py --input rcs.npz --output plots/ --title "Delta Wing RCS"

docs.title: Input Format
docs.text:
The script reads .npz files containing:

data = np.load('rcs.npz')
theta_deg = data['theta']      # theta values (degrees)
phi_deg = data['phi']          # phi values (degrees) - optional
rcs_db = data['rcs']          # RCS in dBsm
frequency = data['frequency']  # frequency in Hz
polarization = data['polarization']  # 'vv' or 'hh'

docs.title: 1D Cut Data
docs.text:
• rcs_cartesian.png - RCS vs theta (Cartesian)
  • rcs_polar.png - RCS polar plot

docs.title: 2D Map Data
docs.text: • rcs_contour.png - 2D theta-phi contour

docs.title: Single Point Data
docs.text: • rcs_single.png - Bar chart for single RCS value

docs.title: Auto-Scaling
docs.text:
All plots use automatic scaling centered on the mean value with 50% deviation:

vmin = mean - mean * 0.5
vmax = mean + mean * 0.5

This ensures the RCS pattern is always clearly visible regardless of the absolute values.

docs.title: Cartesian Plot
docs.text:
• X-axis: Theta (0-180°)
  • Y-axis: RCS (dBsm) with auto-scaling

docs.title: Polar Plot
docs.text:
• Angle: Theta (0-180°)
  • Radius: RCS (dBsm, relative scale)

docs.title: 2D Contour
docs.text:
• X-axis: Phi (0-360°)
  • Y-axis: Theta (0-180°)
  • Color: RCS (dBsm)

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Generate mesh
python nurbs_to_stl.py --shape delta_wing -o wing.stl

# 2. Compute RCS
python bem_rcs.py wing.stl --cut --save rcs.npz

# 3. Plot results
python plot_bem_rcs.py -i rcs.npz -o plots/

This generates:

plots/
├── rcs_cartesian.png
└── rcs_polar.png

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from plot_bem_rcs import (
    plot_rcs_cartesian,
    plot_rcs_polar,
    plot_rcs_2d_contour,
    auto_scale
)

# Load data
data = np.load('rcs.npz')
theta_deg = data['theta']
rcs_db = data['rcs']

# Cartesian plot
plot_rcs_cartesian(theta_deg, rcs_db, 
                   title="RCS Pattern",
                   output_path='rcs_cartesian.png')

# Polar plot  
plot_rcs_polar(theta_deg, rcs_db,
               title="RCS Pattern",
               output_path='rcs_polar.png')

# 2D contour (for map data)
phi_deg = data['phi']
rcs_map = data['rcs']
plot_rcs_2d_contour(theta_deg, phi_deg, rcs_map,
                     title="RCS Map",
                     output_path='rcs_contour.png')

# Auto-scale helper
vmin, vmax = auto_scale(rcs_db)

docs.title: Cartesian Plot
docs.text:
• Line color: Blue
  • Fill: Alpha 0.3
  • Grid: Enabled
  • X-axis: 0-180°
  • Y-axis: Auto-scaled to mean ± 50%

docs.title: Polar Plot
docs.text:
• Relative dB scale from mean
  • Theta zero at North
  • Clockwise direction

docs.title: Contour Plot
docs.text:
• Colormap: Jet
  • Colorbar: dBsm
  • Shading: Gouraud

docs.title: Plot STL Files
docs.text: Visualize STL (Stereolithography) mesh files in 3D.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python plot_stl.py <stl_file> [output_png]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
Argument Description stl_file Path to input STL file (required) output_png Output PNG file path (optional)

If no output path is provided, displays interactively (requires display).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Save to file
python plot_stl.py sphere.stl sphere_plot.png

# View mesh
python plot_stl.py sphere.stl

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Generates a 3D visualization showing: - Mesh surface with triangular facets - Axis labels (X, Y, Z in meters) - Automatic scaling to fit mesh

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D
from stl import mesh

def plot_stl(file_path: str, output_path: str = None):
    """Plot STL mesh in 3D."""
    stl_mesh = mesh.Mesh.from_file(file_path)

    # Create figure with 3D axes
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 10))
    ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

    # Plot triangular surface
    ax.plot_trisurf(x, y, z, triangles=..., alpha=0.7)

    if output_path:
        fig.savefig(output_path, dpi=150)

docs.title: Visualization Options
docs.text: The plot uses: - Alpha: 0.7 (70% opacity) - Color: Steel blue - Edge color: Gray - DPI: 150 for saved images

docs.title: Read STL Files
docs.text: Read and display information from STL (Stereolithography) mesh files.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: python read_stl.py <stl_file>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description stl_file Path to input STL file (required)

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The script displays: - Number of triangles - Total surface area (m²) - Bounding box dimensions (X, Y, Z) - Object dimensions

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ python read_stl.py sphere.stl

Reading: sphere.stl

Mesh Information:
  Number of triangles: 1600
  Total surface area: 3.1255 m²
  Bounding box:
    X: -0.5000 to 0.5000
    Y: -0.5000 to 0.5000
    Z: -0.5000 to 0.5000
  Dimensions:
    X: 1.0000 m
    Y: 1.0000 m
    Z: 1.0000 m

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from stl import mesh

def read_stl(file_path: str) -> mesh.Mesh:
    """Read an STL file and return mesh object."""
    return mesh.Mesh.from_file(file_path)

def get_mesh_info(stl_mesh: mesh.Mesh) -> dict:
    """Extract information from STL mesh."""
    # Returns dict with:
    # - num_triangles
    # - bounding_box
    # - dimensions
    # - total_area

docs.title: STL Format
docs.text:
STL files describe the surface geometry of 3D objects: - ASCII STL: Human-readable text format - Binary STL: More compact binary format

Both formats represent surfaces as a collection of triangular facets.
